**NASA's Parker Solar Probe to Explore the Sun's Secrets**

NASA has announced its ambitious mission to explore the sun's outer atmosphere, which will be named the Parker Solar Probe in honor of astrophysicist Eugene Parker. The spacecraft will be the first to fly directly into the sun's corona, the outer layer of the sun's atmosphere, and will provide unprecedented insights into the sun's behavior and the solar wind.

The Parker Solar Probe will be launched in July 2018 and will make 24 close flybys of the sun, coming within 3.9 million miles of the sun's surface. The spacecraft will have to withstand temperatures of up to 2,500 degrees Fahrenheit and solar radiation intensities 475 times greater than on Earth. The probe will be equipped with a 7.5-foot-wide heat shield made of advanced carbon-composite material to protect its instruments from the extreme heat.

The mission aims to answer two long-standing puzzles: how the solar wind is accelerated and why the sun's outer atmosphere is hotter than its surface. The solar wind is a stream of charged particles that flows from the sun and affects Earth's magnetic field, causing disruptions to satellites and power supplies. Understanding the sun's behavior is crucial for predicting space weather events, which can impact Earth's power grid and radiation exposure on airline flights.

The Parker Solar Probe will carry a chip with photos of Eugene Parker and his revolutionary paper on solar wind, as well as a plate with an inscription of his choice. **Habeas Corpus: A Fundamental Right Under Threat and a New Frontier for Animal Rights**

The writ of habeas corpus, a cornerstone of Western law since the Magna Carta in 1215, has been eroded by the Military Commissions Act (MCA) of 2006. This law, passed in the waning days of the Bush administration, revoked the right to habeas corpus for individuals detained at Guantanamo Bay and for non-citizens labeled as "enemy combatants" anywhere in the world. This provision also applies to legal residents of the US, who could be detained without cause and without access to the courts.

The MCA has been used to justify the indefinite detention of hundreds of individuals at Guantanamo Bay, many of whom were sold into captivity by warlords for bounties. Despite numerous legal victories, the Bush administration has prevented detainees from receiving a fair hearing in the courts. The Supreme Court has agreed to hear the detainees' cases for the third time, in an effort to uphold habeas corpus and hold the administration accountable.

Meanwhile, a new development in the US has brought attention to the writ of habeas corpus in a different context. A New York judge has granted a writ of habeas corpus to two research chimpanzees, Hercules and Leo, held at Stony Brook University. This is the first time in US history that an animal has been granted a writ of habeas corpus, which typically allows human prisoners to challenge their detention. The ruling could force the university to release the chimps and set a precedent for other research animals.

The Nonhuman Rights Project, which filed the case, sees this as a major victory in their efforts to establish the right to bodily liberty for cognitively complex animals. The group plans to use this ruling to sway judges in other jurisdictions and file additional cases, including one involving a captive elephant. The ruling has sparked debate among experts, with some cautioning against reading too much into the decision, while others see it as a significant step forward in recognizing animal rights.

The intersection of these two stories highlights the importance of habeas corpus as a fundamental right that protects individuals from arbitrary detention and ensures that the executive branch is accountable to the courts. The erosion of this right in the context of national security has far-reaching implications for human rights, while the recognition of animal rights through habeas corpus has the potential to revolutionize the way we treat non-human animals in captivity.
answers: The writ of habeas corpus lets prisoners appear in court to make their captors justify why they're being held. Until now in the US, those who've used this legal tactic have been human. But a New York judge yesterday ruled that the writ may be used by two lab chimpanzees at Stony Brook University, Science magazine reports. NY Supreme Court Justice Barbara Jaffe ordered a university rep to address a suit filed by the Nonhuman Rights Project by appearing in court May 6 to argue why the college is holding Hercules and Leo. The group, which says the animals are being detained for biomedical experiments, says its ultimate goal is to have the animals released to the Save the Chimps sanctuary in Fort Pierce, Fla., per the NhRP's website. "This is a big step forward to getting what we are ultimately seeking: the right to bodily liberty for chimpanzees and other cognitively complex animals," Natalie Prosin, the project's executive director, tells Science. The case kicked off in December 2013 with lawsuits that also included two chimps held on private property; each case was struck down, sending the NhRP down the appeals path. Not everyone's convinced the upcoming hearing will mean liberation for the chimps. A Pepperdine University law professor tells Science that the judge likely just wants to hear both sides of the story: "It would be quite surprising if the judge intended to make a momentous substantive finding that chimpanzees are legal persons" without doing so, he notes. Prosin, meanwhile, says her group isn't stopping with the chimps and will also target the release of other animals. (Click to read about what chimps talk to each other about.)

This enrichment is a result of the loss of ordinary hydrogen from the atmosphere over time, which has led to a higher concentration of deuterium in the remaining water. By studying this enrichment, the team was able to estimate the amount of water that was lost from the planet, which was equivalent to a global ocean that would have covered the entire surface of Mars to a depth of 137 meters.

The team's findings suggest that Mars was a habitable planet in the distant past, with long-standing bodies of water, lakes, and deltas. This is a significant departure from previous estimates, which suggested that water on Mars was only present in the form of occasional floods. The discovery of a large ocean on Mars also raises the possibility of life on the planet, as water is a key ingredient for life as we know it.

The research team's findings are supported by other studies, including one that used the Viking 1 orbiter to image the Martian atmosphere and found that the ordinary hydrogen has been leaking into space over time. This has led to a higher proportion of deuterium in the Martian water, which is seven times higher than on Earth.

While the exact amount of water on ancient Mars is still a topic of debate, the evidence suggests that it was a significant fraction of the planet's surface. The discovery of a large ocean on Mars has significant implications for our understanding of the planet's history and the possibility of life on the Red Planet. As NASA's Curiosity rover continues to explore Mars, the search for signs of life on the planet is becoming increasingly promising.

**Key Findings:**

* Ancient Mars had a massive ocean covering nearly half of its northern hemisphere
* The ocean was about a mile deep in some areas and held 20 million cubic kilometers of water
* The water vapor in the Martian atmosphere is enriched

**Dinosaurs' Roars: Separating Fact from Fiction**

The study, led by researchers from the University of Texas, Midwestern University, and the University of Utah, analyzed the vocalizations of over 200 bird species and found that about a quarter of them make "closed-mouth" sounds, which are low, throaty whooshes of air produced by a flesh sac called an esophageal pouch. This type of vocalization is thought to have evolved independently at least 16 times in different bird lineages, and is more common in large-bodied birds, such as doves and ostriches.

The researchers suggest that dinosaurs, being members of the archosaur group, may have had similar vocal abilities, and that some may have made closed-mouth sounds during mating displays. This idea is supported by the fact that many dinosaurs had large body sizes, which would have allowed them to produce the necessary lung pressure to inflate an elastic cavity and produce low-frequency sounds.

In contrast, the sounds used in movies like "Jurassic Park" were created by sound designers using a combination of animal noises, including baby elephant cries, tiger chuffs, and alligator gargles. Gary Rydstrom, the sound designer behind the film, revealed in an interview that he used a variety of animals to create the different dinosaur sounds, including tortoises, horses, geese, and even his own dog, Buster.

The study's findings have significant implications for our understanding of dinosaur behavior and communication. While we may never know exactly what dinosaurs sounded like, the research suggests that they may have been more bird-like than we previously thought, with a range of vocalizations that included closed-mouth sounds. This new perspective on dinosaur vocalizations is likely to change the way we imagine these prehistoric creatures and their behavior.

The car was suspended almost vertically on guide wires attached to a utility pole (see a photo here) in Mendon on Wednesday night, reports the AP. Police say 30-year-old Nabila Altahan of Dorchester, Mass., was headed west on US Route 4 when she passed her intended destination and reacted quickly to the instructions, leaving the road at a significant enough speed to propel the vehicle up the wires. She wasn't injured. (This driver followed her GPS into a lake.)
